attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with three days of progressive shortness of breath, decreased appetite, and confusion. He has not passed urine for 24 hours. He has recently suffered from a gastrointestinal illness which has caused him to have reduced oral intake, as well as significant vomiting and diarrhoea.

He has a medical history of stage 4 chronic kidney disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, and peripheral vascular disease. His medications include insulin, perindopril, bisoprolol, furosemide, and metformin.

Previous discussions about dialysis have been inconclusive, and he has expressed ambivalence about pursuing this treatment. He has seen a palliative care service previously, and he has decided not to be considered for kidney transplantation. No vascular access planning has been undertaken.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 96/min, blood pressure 168/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 92% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. He has bilateral pitting oedema to the mid-thigh, widespread coarse crackles throughout both lung fields, and a raised jugular venous pressure.

Investigations reveal:

Potassium: 6.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)
Creatinine: 876 µmol/L (60-110)
Estimated glomerular filtration rate: 6 mL/min/1.73m² (>90)
pH: 7.22 (7.35-7.45)
Bicarbonate: 16 mmol/L (22-28)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following factors is an absolute contraindication to dialysis in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This case presents an elderly patient with end-stage kidney disease requiring a decision about initiating dialysis. None of the factors mentioned constitute an absolute contraindication to dialysis, and the decision should be based on a comprehensive assessment of the patient's wishes, quality of life, and potential benefits versus burdens of treatment.

The only absolute contraindications to maintenance dialysis would be if vascular access cannot be established, or if there is probative cardiovascular instability. This patient may be offered short term renal replacement therapy/dialysis via a temporary vascular access, to facilitate reversal of acute kidney injury and allow for a definitive discussion regarding his wishes for more permanent therapy.

Age over 75 years alone is not a contraindication to dialysis. While elderly patients may have poorer outcomes on dialysis compared to younger patients, many elderly patients maintain good quality of life on dialysis with appropriate support.

Multiple comorbidities, while potentially affecting prognosis and quality of life, do not absolutely contraindicate dialysis. The presence of comorbidities should inform shared decision-making but does not preclude dialysis if the patient wishes to proceed.

Lack of established vascular access is not a contraindication as temporary access can be established in emergency situations. While permanent access is preferred, temporary central venous catheters can be used to initiate urgent dialysis if needed.

Previous ambivalence towards dialysis necessitates further discussion but is not a contraindication. Many patients require time and support to make this significant decision, and their preferences may change when faced with symptomatic disease.

None of these factors is an absolute contraindication to dialysis. The only absolute contraindications would be patient refusal after informed discussion or situations where dialysis would be futile due to imminent death from another condition. The decision to initiate dialysis should be individualised, considering the patient's values, preferences, and goals of care.
learningPoints: The decision to initiate dialysis should be based on patient preference, quality of life considerations, and overall prognosis rather than age or comorbidities alone.
Lack of vascular access planning does not preclude emergency dialysis via temporary access if clinically indicated and aligned with patient wishes.
Advanced care planning discussions should occur early in chronic kidney disease to avoid crisis decision-making.
references: Chan CT, Blankestijn PJ, Dember LM, Gallieni M, Harris DCH, Lok CE, Mehrotra R, Stevens PE, Wang AY, Cheung M, Wheeler DC, Winkelmayer WC, Pollock CA; Conference Participants. Dialysis initiation, modality choice, access, and prescription: conclusions from a Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) Controversies Conference. Kidney Int. 2019 Jul;96(1):37-47. doi: 10.1016/j.kint.2019.01.017. Epub 2019 Apr 13. PMID: 30987837. Available online at ; https://www.kidney-international.org/article/S0085-2538(19)30138-3/fulltext
